title: Abstract
content: UV irradiation of highly dispersed, anchored titanium oxide catalyst and finely powdered TiO 2 catalysts in the presence of a mixture of CO 2 and H20 led to the formation of (CH 4 CH3OH CO) and CH4, respectively, into the gas phase at 275 K.The efficiency of these photoreactions depended strongly on the kind of catalyst, the ratio of CO 2 to H 2 O, and the reaction temperature.In the case of highly dispersed titanium oxide catalysts, the charge transfer excited state, i.e. (Ti 3-0-) 3 of the tetrahedral coordinated titanium oxide species was found to play an important role in the appearance of a highly efficient reactivity.The anatase-type TiO 2 catalyst with large band gap and numerous surface -OH groups showed high efficiency for the formation of CH 4. Detection of Ti 3, H, C and CH 3 radicals by electron spin resonance at 77 K suggests that the photoreaction of CO 2 with H20 proceeds via the formation of CO and C radicals from CO 2. The Cu-loading on the small powdered TiO 2 catalyst brought about new formation of CH3OH, and the Cu species on TiO 2 were found to play a significant role in this photocatalytic reaction.In the case of single crystals of futile TiO2, the surface of TiO2(100) exhibited a higher efficiency for the formation of CH 4 and CH3OH than TiO2(110).Measurements of the intermediate species formed on the TiO2(100) single crystal were carded out using high resolution electron energy loss spectroscopy.

title: Experimental
content: Titanium oxide anchored on Vycor glass was prepared by an anchoring method.Anchoring was performed using a facile reaction of TiCI 4 with the surface OH groups on the transparent porous Vycor glass (Corning code 7930, BET surface area 150 m 2 g-l) at 473 K, followed by treatment with H20 vapor to hydrolyze the anchored compounds.The titanium content in the anchored catalyst was 0.17 wt..Four different types of powdered TiO 2 catalyst (JRC-TIO-2,3,4,5) supplied by the Catalysis Society of Japan (Table 1) were also used (grain size 0.02-1 xm).Detailed information about these standard TiO 2 catalysts is available from the Catalysis Society of Japan.Cu-loaded TiO 2 catalysts were prepared by the impregnation of TiO 2 (JRC-TIO-4) in an aqueous solution of CuCl 2. Wafers (10 10 1 mm 3) of futile TiO 2 single crystals with (100) and (110) surfaces were used.Pretreatment of the catalysts and the photocatalytic reactions were carried out in a quartz cell with a flat bottom (60 ml) connected to a conventional vacuum system (10 -6 Torr range) 1,2.The catalysts were degassed at 725 K for 2 h, calcined in O 2 at 725 K for 2 h, and finally degassed at 475 K for 2 h before use.UV irradiation of the catalyst in the presence of CO 2 (0.04-0.15 mmol) and H20 (0.04-0.25 mmol) was carried out using a 75 W high-pressure Hg lamp through water and color filters (A 280 nm) at 273-323 K. Products were analyzed by gas chromatography and a mass spectrometer. The photoluminescence and electron spin resonance (ESR) spectra were recorded at 77 K and 295 K.The X-ray photoelectron (XP) spectra were recorded using Mg radiation at room temperature.X-ray absorption fine structure (XAFS) spectra (XANES and EXAFS) were obtained at the BL-7C facility of the Photon Factory at the National Laboratory for High-Energy Physics, Tsukuba.Si(lll) double crystals were used to monochromatize the X-rays from the 2.5 GeV electron storage ring.The Ti K edge absorption spectra were recorded in the fluorescence mode for anchored catalysts or in transmission mode for titanium compounds at 295 K. Fourier transformation (FT) was performed on k3-weighted EXAFS oscillations in the range 3-10 ,-1.HREELS measurements were conducted in a stainless ultrahigh vacuum apparatus (UHV, 10 -1 Torr range) equipped with an analyzer for Auger electron spectroscopy (AES) 8.The TiO2(100) surface was cleaned by repeating the Ar bombardment several times at 295 K as well as annealing and oxidation in 0 2 at 800 K. Further experimental details can be found in our previous literature 1234.The excitation of light at around 280 nm brought about an electron transfer from oxygen to titanium ion, resulting in the formation of pairs of the trapped hole center (O-) and an electron center (Ti 3) 5,6.As shown in Fig. l(b)-(e), the addition of H20 or CO 2 molecules at 275 K on the anchored titanium oxide leads to efficient quenching of the photoluminescence.Such efficient quenching of the photoluminescence with CO 2 or H20 suggests not only that the emitting sites are highly dispersed on the surfaces, but also that added CO 2 or H20 interacts andor reacts with the anchored titanium oxide species in both its ground and excited states.b Conversion in reaction of methyl acetylene with H20.c Conversion in reaction of 2-butene.d As reported previously, there was no correlation between activity of the catalysts and their surface area.Fig. 2 shows the XANES and FT-EXAFS spectra of the titanium oxide catalysts anchored on Vycor glass and bulk TiO 2 (anatase).Although the bulk TiO 2 exhibits three small pre-edge peaks in its XANES spectrum, the anchored titanium oxide catalyst (Fig. 2(a)) exhibits only a single and intense pre-edge peak which is similar to that of tetra-i-propoxy titanium with tetrahedral coordination, indicating the presence of tetrahedrally coordinated titanium oxide species on the surfaces.The FT-EXAFS spectra of the anchored titanium oxide catalyst (Fig. 2(A UV irradiation of the anchored titanium oxide catalysts in the presence of a mixture of CO 2 and H20 led to the evolution of CH 4, CH3OH, and CO in the gas phase at 323 K, as well as trace amounts of C2H 4 and C2H 6.The evolution of small amounts of 0 2 was also observed.However, no products could be detected under dark conditions.Fig. 3 shows the time profiles for the production of CH 4 in relation to the UV irradiation time.The yields of the products are in a good linear relationship with the irradiation time for a long period up to 6-8 h, and then decline gradually, especially after a long UV irradiation time.The total yields of CH 4, CH3OH and CO were larger under UV irradiation at 323 K than at 275 K.It is clear that the photocatalytic reactions proceed more efficiently at higher temperatures.Fig. 3 also shows the effect of the H20 to CO 2 ratio of the photocatalytic reactivity.As shown in Fig. 3, the efficiency of the photocatalytic reaction depends strongly on the ratio of H20 to CO 2 and its reactivity increases with an increase in the ratio of H20 to CO2; however, an excess amount of H20 suppresses the reaction rates.UV irradiation of the anchored titanium oxide catalysts in the presence of CO 2 and H20 at 77 K led to the appearance of ESR signals attributed to C radicals, H atoms and Ti 3 ions.After the disappearance of these ESR signals in this system at around 275 K, the formation of CH 4 and CH3OH was observed.At 77 K, the ESR signal assigned to the 0 2 species was observed in the reaction system.The formation of graphitic carbon species was also observed by XPS study of the anchored titanium oxide catalyst after the photoreaction.Therefore, graphitic carbon species and 0 2 species are formed and stimulated on the surface, which may be responsible for the decrease in catalytic activity after longer reaction times.

title: Results and discussion
content: Scheme 1 shows the reaction mechanism of the photocatalytic reduction of CO 2 with H20 on the anchored titanium oxide catalyst.CO 2 and H20 molecules interact with the excited state of photoinduced (Ti3-O-) species and the reduction of CO 2 and the decomposition of H20 proceed competitively, depending on the ratio of H20 to CO 2. These interactions result in the formation of H atoms, OH radicals and carbon species, and these intermediate radical species react with each other to form CH 4 and CH3OH. UV irradiation of the powdered TiO 2 catalysts in the presence of a mixture of CO 2 and H20 led to the evolution of CH 4 into the gas phase at 275 K. Trace amounts of C2H 4 and C;H 6 were also produced.The yields of these products increased with the UV irradiation time, while no products were detected under dark conditions.The CH 4 yield was almost zero in the reaction of CO 2 without H20 and increased with increasing amounts of H20.These results suggest that the photocatalytic reduction of CO 2 to produce CH 4 and C 2 compounds from CO 2 and H20 takes place photocatalytically in the solid-gas phase systems with the powdered TiO 2 catalysts.Such photocatalytic production of CH 4 from CO 2 and H20 has also been reported by Hemminger et al. for the PtSrTiO 3 catalyst 9.However, Inoue et al. 10 have reported that HCOOH, HCHO, and CHaOH are produced under the illumination of aqueous suspension systems involving a variety of semiconductor powders such as TiO 2 and SrTiO 3. Recently, Ishitani et al. 11 reported that deposition of metals (Pd, Pt, Rh, etc) onto TiO 2 accelerated considerably the photocatalytic reduction of CO 2 to CH 4 and CH3CO2H.The pioneering works on the photoreduction of CO 2 on semiconductors were summarized by Halmann 12,13. The CH 4 yields on the four types of TiO 2 catalyst are shown in Table 1.The level of photocatalytic reactivity is found to depend on the type of TiO 2 catalyst in the order JRC-TIO-4 -5 -2 -3.The photocatalytic reactivities of these four TiO 2 catalysts were also investigated for the hydrogenation reaction of methyl acetylene with H202 14 and for the isomerization of 2-butene 14.These findings are also shown in Table 1.The trend in catalytic activity for the photocatalytic reduction of CO 2 with H20 is similar to that for the hydrogenation and isomerization of olefins, proving that the reduction of CO 2 with H20 occurs photocatalytically over the powdered TiO 2 catalyst.The various chemical and physical properties of these four TiO 2 catalysts are also shown in Table 1.From these results, it is likely that the anatase-type TiO 2, which has a large band gap and numerous surface -OH groups, is preferable for efficient photocatalytic reactions.The increase in band gap is accompanied by a shift in the conduction band edge to higher energy levels.This shift causes the reductive potential to shift to more negative values which in turn causes a great enhancement in the photocatalytic reactivity.This effect seems to be more crucial for difficult or slow reactions such as the reduction of CO 2 with H20. Concerning the role of the surface -OH groups, the surface -OH groups andor physisorbed H20 play a significant role in photocatalytic reactions via the formation of -OH radicals and H radicals.Thus, the data shown in Table 1 can be interpreted in terms of the small changes in band gap andor the concentration of surface -OH groups.The small amount of oxygen produced may be associated with the adsorption of oxygen onto the TiO 2 surfaces in the presence of H20 15.Fig. 4 shows the ESR signals obtained under UV irradiation of the anatase-type TiO 2 (JRC-TIO-4) catalyst in the presence of CO 2 and H20 at 77 K.The ESR signals are attributed to the characteristic photogenerated Ti 3 ions (g 1.9723 and g II 1.9628) and H radicals (with 490 G splitting), as well as CH 3 radicals which consist of four lines with intensity ratios 1 : 3 : 3 : 1, having a hyperfine splitting Ha----19.2G and g-value of 2.002.The signal intensity of CH 3 radicals decreased with increasing amount of H20, indicating that CH 3 radicals react easily tO form CH 4 in the presence of enough H20.These results clearly suggest that CH 3 radicals are the intermediate species and react with H radicals that are formed by the reduction of protons (H ) supplied from H20 adsorbed on the catalyst. Yields of the formation of CH 4 and CH3OH in the photocatalytic reduction of CO 2 (124.molg-l) with H20 (372 p.mol g-t) at 275 K Single crystal Yield nmol h-i g-cat-I CH4 CH3OH TiO2(100) 3.5 2.4 TiO2(110) 0 0.8 The photocatalytic reduction of CO 2 with H20 was also nvestigated on CuTiO 2 catalysts.Although the CuTiO 2 catalyst was less effective for the photocatalytic production of CH 4, new formation of CH3OH was observed on the CuTiO a catalyst.CuTiO 2 catalysts with a Cu loading ,)f 0.3-1.0wt. exhibited photocatalytic reactivity to produce CHaOH with a yield of 0.006-0.004tzmol g-cat -l.However, the addition of excess Cu to TiO 2 was undesirable for the formation of CH3OH.Fig. 5 shows the Cu(2Pa2.j2)and the Cu(L3VV) XPS spectra of the CuTiO 2 catalyst just before the photo-reaction.The abience of the satellite peak in the Cu(2p) spectrum and the position of the Cu(L3VV) peak suggest that the main pecies of Cu in the catalyst is Cu .Recently, it has been reported that Cu catalysts play a significant role in the photoelectrochemical production of CH3OH from the CO 2 and H20 system 16.The results obtained in the present itudy correspond well with these reports.After the photocatalytic reduction the CuTiO 2 catalyst exhibited a new peak at 299 eV in the C(ls) XPS spectrum.The appear- Kinetic Energy e V Fig. 5. XPS spectra of CuTiO 2 catalyst after irradiation in the presence of CO 2 (0.12 mmol) and H20 (0.37 mmol) for 4 h. ance of this new peak suggests that carboxylate groups are formed on the catalyst during the photocatalytic reaction.These carboxylate groups which accumulated on the catalyst may be the primary intermediate species for the photocatalytic reduction of CO with H20. UV irradiation of the TiO2(100) surface in the presence of a mixture of CO 2 and H20 led to the evolution of CH 4 and CHaOH in the gas phase at 275 K, whereas only CH3OH was detected with the TiO2(ll0) single-crystal catalyst.The yields of the photo-formed CH 4 and CH3OH increased linearly with the UV irradiation time, indicating that the photoinduced reduction of CO 2 in the presence of H20 to produce CH 4 and CH3OH takes place photocatalytically on the surfaces of TiO 2 single crystals. The yields of the formation of CH 4 and CH3OH on the two types of TiO 2 single crystal are shown in Table 2.It is clear that the photocatalytic reactivity and selectivity depend strongly on the type of TiO 2 single-crystal surface.The yield of CHaOH formation is much higher on TiO2(100) than on TiO2(110), while the formation of CH 4 is only observed on TiO2(100) and not on TiO2(110).It is likely that the photo-formed electrons localize on the surface sites of the excited TiO 2 to play a significant role in the photoreduction of CO 2 molecules to intermediate carbon species on the TiO 2 surfaces 123.The surface Ti atoms may act as an electron moiety on the surfaces, i.e. a reductive site.According to the surface geometric models for TiO2(100) and TiO2(110), the atomic ratio (TiO) of top-surface Ti and O atoms with geometric spaces large enough to have direct contact with CO 2 and H20 molecules is higher on TiO2(100) than TiO2(ll0).In the excited state, the surface with a higher TiO surface ratio, i.e.TiO2(100), exhibits a more reductive property than TiO2(ll0).Such a reductive surface allows more facile reduction of CO 2 molecules, especially for the formation of CH 4 which was found to be a vital step in the photoreduction of CO 2 with H20 on the TiO 2 catalyst. To clarify the intermediate species formed in the photocatalytic reduction of CO 2 with HE0 , the surface species on TiO2(100), which exhibits a high efficiency in the photoreduction of CO 2 with H20, were investigated using HREELS because of its extremely high sensitivity.Fig. 6 shows typical HREELS spectra of a clean TiO2(100) sin- gle crystal before (a) and after UV irradiation for 5 min in the presence of CO 2 (1 Tort) and gaseous H20 (3 Torr) (b) respectively.With the clean surface (a), no HREELS peak is observed except for peaks due to the optical phonon of the TiO 2 lattice at 680 cm -t and its weak harmonic oscillation.However, with the surface after UV irradiation (b), two peaks are detected at 2920 and 3630 cm -1 in addition to the phonon peak, which can be assigned to the C-H stretching vibration of CH x species 8 and to O-H stretching of the surface hydroxyl groups respectively 17.However, without UV irradiation only a weak peak assigned to the OH stretching vibration was observed; on exposure of the catalyst to reactant gases no other peaks were detected, indicating that UV irradiation is indispensable for the reduction of CO2 molecules and the formation of active H and CH x species.

title: Conclusions
content: UV irradiation of the various active titanium oxide catalysts in the presence of CO 2 and H20 at 275 K led to the photocatalytic reduction of CO 2 to produce CH 4, CH3OH and CO as the main products.The yields of the photocatalytic reactions depended strongly on the kind of catalyst, the ratio of CO 2 to H20 and the reaction temperature.In situ spectroscopic study of the system indicated that the photocatalytic reduction of CO 2 with H20 is linked to the formation of reductive TiO 2 surface sites by UV irradiation and a much higher reactivity of the charge transfer excited state, i.e. (Ti 3 -O-)3 of the tetrahedral coordinated titanium oxide species formed on the surface.Based on the detection of intermediate reaction species such as Ti 3, H atoms, C radicals, CH 3 radicals and CH x species, the reaction mechanism for the photocatalytic reduction of CO 2 with H20 has been characterized at the molecular level. Fig. I. Photoluminescence spectra of the anchored titanium oxide at 77K (a), and the quenching by added CO 2 (b) and H20 (c)-(e).Excitation beam 280 nm; amount of added CO2, (b) 0.6 mmol g-t ; amounts of added H20, (c) 0.04, (d) 0.08, (e) 0.2 mmol g-i. Fig. l(a) shows a typical photoluminescence spectrum of the titanium oxide anchored on Vycor glass at 77 K.The excitation of light at around 280 nm brought about an electron transfer from oxygen to titanium ion, resulting in the formation of pairs of the trapped hole center (O-) and an electron center (Ti 3)5,6.As shown in Fig.l(b)-(e), the addition of H20 or CO 2 molecules at 275 K on the anchored titanium oxide leads to efficient quenching of the photoluminescence.Such efficient quenching of the photoluminescence with CO 2 or H20 suggests not only that the emitting sites are highly dispersed on the surfaces, but also that added CO 2 or H20 interacts andor reacts with the anchored titanium oxide species in both its ground and excited states. Fig.2.Ti K edge XANES spectra (left) and FT-EXAFS spectra (right) of the titanium oxide catalysts anchored on Vycor glass (a, A), bulk TiO2 (anatase) (b, B) and tetra-i-propexy titanium (c.C). Fig.2shows the XANES and FT-EXAFS spectra of the titanium oxide catalysts anchored on Vycor glass and bulk TiO 2 (anatase).Although the bulk TiO 2 exhibits three small pre-edge peaks in its XANES spectrum, the anchored titanium oxide catalyst (Fig.2(a)) exhibits only a single and intense pre-edge peak which is similar to that of tetra-i-propoxy titanium with tetrahedral coordination, indicating the presence of tetrahedrally coordinated titanium oxide species on the surfaces.The FT-EXAFS spectra of the anchored titanium oxide catalyst (Fig.2(A)) exhibits only Ti-O peaks, indicating the presence of an isolated titanium oxide species.These results indicate that highly Scheme 1. Schematic representation of the photocatalytic reduction of CO 2 with H20 on the anchored titanium oxide. Fig. 4. ESR signals obtained with TiO 2 under UV irradiation in the presence of CO 2 (0.12 mmol) and H20 (0.37 mmol) at 77 K (irradiation time 6 h). Fig. 6.HREEL spectra of TiO2(100) before and after UV irradiation: (a) clean surface, (b) after UV irradiation for 5 min in the presence of CO 2 (1 Torr) and gaseous H20 (3 Torr).(P, phonon peak; P-P, phonon-phonon peak). a CH 4 yield in reaction of CO 2 with H20.
